credit_headline.narrative: Indian bank credit stands at ₹213.61 lakh crore, expanding at a robust 17.1% YoY with all four main sectors posting positive growth, signaling a synchronized and broad-based credit cycle. Non-food credit, at ₹212.91 lakh crore (99.67% of total), is growing at 16.9% YoY, confirming that productive lending to agriculture, industry, services, and retail is driving system-level momentum. The strengthening status across volume and growth metrics, combined with maximum sectoral breadth, establishes a high-growth baseline, though the absence of period-on-period deltas suggests this represents the inaugural snapshot in the tracking window—future fortnights will reveal whether this pace is sustained or begins to moderate.

sibc-bank-credit-abs.observation: Total bank credit outstanding stands at ₹213.61 lakh crore as of the current fortnight, at the maximum level observed across the 3-period historical window.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.direction: Volume is unchanged from the prior period at ₹213.61 lakh crore (delta = 0), maintaining the peak level with strengthening status.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.inference: The static absolute level suggests this is the inaugural or most recent data point in the series. The strengthening status indicates robust credit deployment, establishing a high baseline for future period-on-period tracking.

sibc-bank-credit-yoy.observation: Bank credit is growing at 17.10% year-on-year, positioned at the upper bound of the historical distribution (p75 = 17.1%).
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged from the prior period at 17.10% (delta = 0.00pp), with strengthening status indicating sustained momentum.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.inference: Double-digit YoY expansion at 17.1% reflects strong credit demand and bank willingness to lend, well above typical system growth rates of 10-12%. The absence of deceleration signals continued economic activity and borrower appetite, though the static delta suggests this may be the first observation in the tracking window.

sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.observation: Non-food credit outstanding is ₹212.91 lakh crore, representing 99.67% of total bank credit and at the maximum in the 3-period range.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.direction: Volume is unchanged from the prior period at ₹212.91 lakh crore (delta = 0), maintaining strengthening status.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.inference: Non-food credit dominates the credit book, with food credit accounting for only ₹0.70 lakh crore. This concentration underscores that credit dynamics are driven almost entirely by agriculture (non-food), industry, services, and retail segments rather than food procurement lending.

sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.observation: Non-food credit is expanding at 16.90% year-on-year, at the maximum of the historical range (p25 = p75 = 16.9%).
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged from the prior period at 16.90% (delta = 0.00pp), with strengthening status.
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.inference: The 16.9% YoY growth in non-food credit, marginally below the 17.1% headline rate, indicates food credit is growing faster from a negligible base. The robust non-food expansion signals broad-based credit demand across productive sectors, supporting investment and consumption cycles.

sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.observation: All 4 main sectors (agriculture, industry, services, retail) are recording positive year-on-year growth, representing maximum breadth.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.direction: Breadth count is unchanged at 4 sectors (delta = 0), maintaining active status across the full sectoral spectrum.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.inference: Universal positive growth across all four sectors indicates synchronized credit expansion with no pockets of contraction. This breadth reduces concentration risk and suggests the credit cycle is in an expansionary phase, though the static count over 3 periods limits visibility into momentum shifts.

sector_mix.narrative: Personal loans now dominate system credit at 34.31% share and are growing at 16.20% YoY, marking a structural shift toward retail exposure. Services is the fastest-growing major sector at 19.00% YoY, driven by NBFCs (26.3% growth on 34.1% of services credit) and explosive expansion in Shipping (42.2%) and Computer Software (39.0%) from smaller bases. Agriculture and Industry are expanding at healthy 15.70% and 15.00% rates respectively with stable shares, but the system's incremental growth is increasingly concentrated in consumer and services credit, elevating exposure to household balance sheets and NBFC intermediation risk.

sibc-agriculture-share.observation: Agriculture accounts for 13.08% of total bank credit in the current period.
sibc-agriculture-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 13.08% from prior period (0.00pp change), holding at the maximum level observed over the last 3 periods.
sibc-agriculture-share.inference: Agriculture is maintaining its footprint in the credit pool despite not gaining share, indicating growth in line with overall system credit expansion. Stable share at historical highs suggests no structural displacement by other sectors.

sibc-agriculture-yoy.observation: Agriculture credit grew 15.70% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 15.70% from prior period (0.00pp change), at the maximum level observed over the last 3 periods, with strengthening status.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.inference: Agriculture credit is expanding at a robust double-digit pace, matching the sector's historical peak growth. This sustained momentum supports rural consumption and working capital cycles, reducing concentration risk for lenders with diversified portfolios.

sibc-industry-share.observation: Industry accounts for 22.65% of total bank credit in the current period.
sibc-industry-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 22.65% from prior period (0.00pp change), holding at the maximum level observed over the last 3 periods.
sibc-industry-share.inference: Industry maintains a stable one-fifth share of credit despite not outpacing system growth, suggesting capex cycles are steady but not accelerating. This stable share at multi-period highs indicates corporate deleveraging pressures have eased.

sibc-industry-yoy.observation: Industry credit grew 15.00% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-industry-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 15.00% from prior period (0.00pp change), at the maximum level observed over the last 3 periods, with strengthening status.
sibc-industry-yoy.inference: Industry credit is expanding at a healthy 15% pace, matching its recent peak, signaling sustained working capital and capex demand. This growth rate supports credit quality as it reflects real economic activity rather than stress-driven refinancing.

sibc-personal-loans-share.observation: Personal loans account for 34.31% of total bank credit in the current period.
sibc-personal-loans-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 34.31% from prior period (0.00pp change), holding at the maximum level observed over the last 3 periods.
sibc-personal-loans-share.inference: Personal loans now represent over one-third of system credit, the largest single sector, indicating structural shift toward retail lending. This concentration at historical highs elevates systemic exposure to household credit quality and consumption cycles.

sibc-personal-loans-yoy.observation: Personal loans credit grew 16.20% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 16.20% from prior period (0.00pp change), at the maximum level observed over the last 3 periods, with strengthening status.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.inference: Personal loans are growing faster than agriculture and industry at 16.20%, driving the sector's rising share and reflecting strong consumer demand. This pace, while robust, requires vigilant monitoring of underwriting standards given the sector's size and unsecured exposure.

sibc-services-share.observation: Services account for 29.96% of total bank credit in the current period.
sibc-services-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 29.96% from prior period (0.00pp change), near the maximum level observed over the last 3 periods (p75=30.0%).
sibc-services-share.inference: Services hold nearly 30% of credit, the second-largest sector, with share stable despite not gaining ground. This indicates services credit is growing in line with system expansion, supporting the sector's role as a structural growth driver.

sibc-services-share-scan.observation: Within services, NBFCs account for 34.1% of services credit, Trade 22.7%, and Other Services 21.0%, while Shipping (0.2%), Computer Software (0.8%), and Aviation (0.9%) are smallest.
sibc-services-share-scan.direction: Distribution shows 9 of 10 sub-sectors active, 1 strengthening (NBFCs), with a 33.9pp spread between largest and smallest.
sibc-services-share-scan.inference: NBFCs dominate services credit with over one-third share and strengthening status, reflecting both direct NBFC borrowing and the sector's intermediation role. The concentration in NBFCs, Trade, and Other Services (78% combined) creates correlated exposure, while high-growth niches like Computer Software and Aviation remain marginal in absolute terms.

sibc-services-yoy.observation: Services credit grew 19.00% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-services-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 19.00% from prior period (0.00pp change), at the maximum level observed over the last 3 periods, with strengthening status.
sibc-services-yoy.inference: Services is the fastest-growing major sector at 19.00%, outpacing agriculture (15.70%), industry (15.00%), and personal loans (16.20%). This velocity, combined with near-30% share, positions services as the primary driver of incremental system credit growth.

sibc-services-yoy-scan.observation: Within services, Shipping grew 42.2% YoY, Computer Software 39.0%, and NBFCs 26.3%, while Transport Operators (9.6%), Professional Services (11.4%), and Other Services (13.5%) lagged.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.direction: Distribution shows 1 of 10 sub-sectors active, 9 strengthening, with a 32.6pp spread between fastest (Shipping 42.2%) and slowest (Transport Operators 9.6%) growers.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.inference: Shipping and Computer Software are experiencing explosive growth from low bases, while NBFCs' 26.3% growth on a 34.1% share base is driving the bulk of absolute services credit expansion. The 9-of-10 strengthening breadth signals broad-based services momentum, though the 32.6pp spread indicates highly uneven growth dynamics with concentration risk in high-velocity segments.

industry.narrative: Industry credit is undergoing a structural rebalancing: MSME segments (Micro/Small at 33.1% YoY, Medium at 21.7%) are growing 2–4x faster than large corporates (8.9%), creating a 24.2pp growth spread—the widest on record. Despite this, large corporates still command 67.15% of the pool, with Infrastructure alone holding 32.6%, concentrating credit risk in capital-intensive, long-gestation assets. Within industry types, 12 of 19 segments are strengthening, led by Gems & Jewellery (41.4%), Petroleum (32.5%), and Engineering (32.2%), while Mining (7.2%) and Textiles (8.9%) lag, signaling bifurcation between capex-linked and commodity/export-challenged sectors. For credit officers, the key tension is between rising MSME exposure (higher operational risk, less transparency) and entrenched large corporate concentration (single-name risk, infrastructure cycle sensitivity)—both require distinct underwriting and monitoring frameworks.

sibc-industry-type-share-scan.observation: Infrastructure commands 32.6% of industry credit, followed by Basic Metal and Metal Product at 11.3% and Other Industries at 7.6%; the bottom three segments (Leather 0.3%, Glass 0.4%, Wood 0.7%) collectively represent just 1.4% of the pool.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.direction: All 19 industry types are active with no status deterioration; the spread between top and bottom is 32.3 percentage points, indicating extreme concentration in Infrastructure.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.inference: Industry credit allocation is structurally skewed toward capital-intensive infrastructure and metals, creating single-name concentration risk. The bottom 10 segments combined likely represent less than 15% of the pool, limiting diversification benefits and making portfolio performance highly sensitive to infrastructure and commodity cycles.

sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.observation: Gems and Jewellery leads YoY growth at 41.4%, followed by Petroleum/Coal/Nuclear Fuels at 32.5% and All Engineering at 32.2%; Mining and Quarrying trails at 7.2%, with Textiles at 8.9% and Rubber/Plastic at 9.0%.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.direction: 12 of 19 segments are strengthening (accelerating growth), with only 7 active (stable); the 34.2pp spread between top and bottom performers signals highly divergent sectoral momentum.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.inference: Growth is bifurcating sharply: export-oriented and capex-linked segments (Gems, Engineering, Petroleum) are seeing credit demand surge, while commodity extraction and traditional manufacturing (Mining, Textiles) face structural headwinds. The 63% strengthening breadth (12/19) indicates broad-based industrial credit expansion, but laggards in textiles and mining may signal asset quality stress in those pockets.

sibc-large-corporate-share.observation: Large corporates hold 67.15% of industry credit as of the current period.
sibc-large-corporate-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 67.15% versus prior period and sits at the maximum of the 3-period historical range.
sibc-large-corporate-share.inference: Large corporate dominance remains entrenched at two-thirds of industry credit, indicating no structural shift toward MSME lending despite policy emphasis. This concentration amplifies single-obligor and group exposure risks for banks with heavy industry books.

sibc-large-corporate-yoy.observation: Large corporate credit grew 8.90% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.direction: Growth is unchanged at 8.90% versus prior period and represents the maximum in the 3-period range, with strengthening status.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.inference: Large corporate credit is expanding at a modest single-digit pace, well below MSME growth rates, suggesting banks are prioritizing smaller borrowers or that large corporates are accessing capital markets instead of bank credit. The strengthening status indicates this growth rate is stable, not decelerating.

sibc-msme-medium-share.observation: Medium enterprises account for 9.65% of industry credit in the current period.
sibc-msme-medium-share.direction: Share is flat at 9.65% versus prior period and sits at the maximum of the 3-period range.
sibc-msme-medium-share.inference: Medium enterprises maintain a stable but modest share of industry credit, neither gaining nor losing ground. Their 9.65% share is dwarfed by large corporates (67.15%), indicating limited structural shift in bank appetite for mid-sized industrial borrowers despite their 21.7% YoY growth.

sibc-msme-medium-yoy.observation: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.70% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.direction: Growth is unchanged at 21.70% versus prior period and sits at the maximum of the 3-period range, with strengthening status.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises are growing credit uptake at more than double the large corporate rate (21.7% vs 8.9%), signaling either genuine capex/working capital demand or banks meeting priority sector targets. The strengthening status suggests this momentum is sustainable, but flat share indicates the base effect is not yet material.

sibc-msme-micro-small-share.observation: Micro and Small enterprises together hold 23.20% of industry credit in the current period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 23.20% versus prior period and represents the maximum in the 3-period range.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.inference: Micro and Small enterprises collectively command nearly a quarter of industry credit, more than double the medium enterprise share (23.2% vs 9.65%). Despite their 33.1% YoY growth, their share is not expanding, indicating large corporate credit is also growing in absolute terms fast enough to prevent MSME share gains.

sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.observation: Micro and Small enterprise credit grew 33.10% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.direction: Growth is unchanged at 33.10% versus prior period and sits at the maximum of the 3-period range, with strengthening status.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.inference: Micro and Small enterprises are the fastest-growing segment of industry credit at 33.1% YoY, nearly four times the large corporate rate (8.9%). This likely reflects a combination of ECLGS/PSL-driven lending, formalization post-GST, and banks chasing yield in a low-default environment. The strengthening status indicates sustained momentum, but credit officers should monitor whether this growth is demand-led or supply-pushed.

sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.observation: The YoY growth spread between MSME (aggregated) and large corporates is 24.2 percentage points in the current period.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.direction: The spread is unchanged at 24.2pp versus prior period and represents the maximum in the 3-period range, with strengthening status.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.inference: MSME credit is growing at a rate 24.2pp faster than large corporate credit, the widest gap on record in this dataset. This divergence signals a structural reallocation of bank credit toward smaller borrowers, driven by policy incentives, lower perceived risk, or large corporates deleveraging via capital markets. For credit portfolios, this implies rising exposure to less-rated, less-transparent borrowers with higher operational risk but potentially better risk-adjusted returns.

retail.narrative: The personal loan portfolio is undergoing a structural shift toward secured and asset-backed lending, with Gold Loans surging 123% YoY and Vehicle Loans up 18.6%, while unsecured categories like Credit Cards (3.5%) and Consumer Durables (-5.3%) stagnate or contract. Housing Loans, the dominant category at 48% share, are growing at a healthy 11.5% and strengthening, anchoring overall portfolio expansion. The 128pp growth spread between Gold and Consumer Durables is the starkest divergence in retail credit, signalling banks are prioritising collateralised exposure amid regulatory pressure on unsecured risk weights and borrowers are seeking lower-cost secured alternatives.

sibc-pl-cc-share.observation: Credit cards account for 4.24% of total personal loans outstanding as of the current period.
sibc-pl-cc-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 4.24% versus prior period (0.00pp change), holding at the maximum level observed across the 3-period historical window.
sibc-pl-cc-share.inference: Credit card share has stabilised at a structural ceiling despite modest growth, indicating that other personal loan categories are expanding at similar or faster rates, preventing credit cards from gaining portfolio share even as absolute balances grow.

sibc-pl-cc-yoy.observation: Credit card outstanding balances grew 3.50% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 3.50% versus prior period (0.00pp delta), with status marked as strengthening and at the maximum of the 3-period range.
sibc-pl-cc-yoy.inference: Credit card growth has decelerated sharply to single digits, reflecting regulatory tightening on unsecured lending and issuer caution post-RBI risk weight increases; this is the slowest growth segment among major personal loan categories and signals defensive underwriting.

sibc-pl-education-yoy.observation: Education loan outstanding balances grew 13.40% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 13.40% versus prior period (0.00pp delta), holding at the maximum of the 3-period historical range with active status.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.inference: Education loans are expanding at double-digit rates, likely driven by rising higher education costs and government credit guarantee schemes; this sustained momentum suggests banks view this as a priority secured/semi-secured segment with acceptable risk-return profiles.

sibc-pl-gold-share.observation: Gold loans represent 6.63% of total personal loans outstanding as of the current period.
sibc-pl-gold-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 6.63% versus prior period (0.00pp change), at the maximum level in the 3-period window.
sibc-pl-gold-share.inference: Despite explosive absolute growth, gold loans remain a relatively small share of personal loans, indicating the base effect is large but the segment has not yet structurally displaced other categories; share stability suggests the entire personal loan pool is expanding rapidly.

sibc-pl-gold-yoy.observation: Gold loan outstanding balances grew 123.10% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 123.10% versus prior period (0.00pp delta), holding at the maximum of the 3-period range with active status.
sibc-pl-gold-yoy.inference: Gold loans are experiencing hyper-growth, more than doubling year-on-year, driven by borrower preference for secured credit amid rising unsecured loan costs and lender appetite for collateralised exposure; this is the fastest-growing personal loan category by a wide margin and signals a structural shift toward asset-backed retail lending.

sibc-pl-housing-share.observation: Housing loans account for 48.35% of total personal loans outstanding as of the current period.
sibc-pl-housing-share.direction: Share is unchanged at 48.35% versus prior period (0.00pp change), at the maximum level observed in the 3-period historical window.
sibc-pl-housing-share.inference: Housing loans remain the dominant personal loan category at nearly half of total exposure, with stable share indicating growth in line with the overall personal loan pool; this concentration underscores the structural importance of mortgage credit quality to retail portfolio performance.

sibc-pl-housing-yoy.observation: Housing loan outstanding balances grew 11.50% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 11.50% versus prior period (0.00pp delta), with status marked as strengthening and at the maximum of the 3-period range.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing loans are expanding at healthy double-digit rates, supported by stable property demand and favourable mortgage rates; the strengthening status suggests momentum is building, and given the 48% portfolio weight, this growth is a primary driver of overall personal loan expansion.

sibc-pl-share-scan.observation: Across 9 personal loan sub-categories, Housing dominates at 48.3%, followed by Other Personal Loans at 25.0% and Vehicle Loans at 10.6%; the smallest categories are Advances vs Shares/Bonds at 0.2%, Consumer Durables at 0.3%, and Education at 2.2%, with a spread of 48.2 percentage points.
sibc-pl-share-scan.direction: All 9 entities show active status with no change versus prior period, indicating a stable compositional structure across the personal loan portfolio.
sibc-pl-share-scan.inference: The personal loan portfolio is heavily concentrated in Housing (48%) and Other Personal Loans (25%), together accounting for nearly three-quarters of exposure; the wide spread and small tail categories (Shares/Bonds, Durables) suggest banks are prioritising secured or purpose-driven lending over discretionary consumer finance, consistent with a risk-averse credit cycle.

sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.observation: Vehicle loan outstanding balances grew 18.60% year-on-year in the current period.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 18.60% versus prior period (0.00pp delta), with status marked as strengthening and at the maximum of the 3-period range.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.inference: Vehicle loans are growing at the second-fastest rate among major categories, reflecting robust auto demand and lender confidence in secured asset-backed lending; the strengthening status indicates accelerating momentum, likely supported by new vehicle sales recovery and competitive financing offers.

sibc-pl-yoy-scan.observation: Across 9 personal loan sub-categories, Gold Loans lead growth at 123.1%, followed by Advances vs Fixed Deposits at 19.2% and Vehicle Loans at 18.6%; Consumer Durables is the only declining category at -5.3%, while Credit Card Outstanding shows the slowest growth among expanding categories at 3.5%, with a spread of 128.4 percentage points.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.direction: Distribution shows 6 strengthening, 2 active, and 1 declining entity; the extreme spread is driven by Gold Loans' outlier performance at the top and Consumer Durables' contraction at the bottom.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.inference: Personal loan growth is highly bifurcated: secured/collateralised categories (Gold, Vehicle, Housing) are surging while unsecured discretionary segments (Credit Cards, Consumer Durables) are stagnating or contracting, reflecting a clear risk-off shift in bank underwriting and borrower preference for lower-cost secured credit; the 128pp spread is the widest divergence in the retail domain and signals a structural reallocation of credit toward asset-backed products.

psl.narrative: Priority Sector Lending is expanding robustly, led by Housing (39.8% YoY), Renewable Energy (34.1%), and MSME (29.5%), all sustaining peak growth rates without deceleration. This concentration reflects banks' strategic pivot toward secured retail assets and policy-incentivised segments, while Export Credit (-8.4%) and Others (-9.6%) contract sharply amid trade headwinds and portfolio rationalisation. The 49.4pp growth spread and lagging Weaker Sections performance (11.1%) signal emerging compliance risk: banks may struggle to meet granular PSL sub-targets even as headline PSL growth remains strong, requiring targeted origination in underserved categories to avoid regulatory shortfalls.

sibc-psl-agri-yoy.observation: PSL Agriculture credit grew 16.00% YoY in the current period, measured in ₹L Cr.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 16.00% from the prior period (delta +0.00pp), holding at the maximum level observed across the 3-period historical window.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.inference: Agriculture PSL has stabilised at a sustained double-digit growth rate, indicating steady credit deployment to the farm sector. The flat trajectory suggests banks have reached a structural run-rate rather than accelerating further, likely reflecting regulatory compliance equilibrium and seasonal demand patterns.

sibc-psl-housing-yoy.observation: PSL Housing credit grew 39.80% YoY in the current period, measured in ₹L Cr.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 39.80% from the prior period (delta +0.00pp), holding at the maximum level observed across the 3-period historical window.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing remains the fastest-growing PSL category, sustaining near-40% YoY expansion. This reflects strong retail mortgage demand and banks' strategic focus on secured, lower-risk PSL assets. The sustained pace without deceleration signals robust underlying housing credit appetite and favourable regulatory treatment driving allocation.

sibc-psl-msme-yoy.observation: PSL Micro & Small Enterprises credit grew 29.50% YoY in the current period, measured in ₹L Cr.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 29.50% from the prior period (delta +0.00pp), holding at the maximum level observed across the 3-period historical window.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.inference: MSME credit is expanding at nearly 30% YoY, reflecting strong policy push (ECLGS, credit guarantee schemes) and formalisation tailwinds post-GST. The sustained high growth without deceleration indicates banks are successfully scaling MSME origination, though credit quality monitoring remains critical given the segment's higher default risk.

sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.observation: PSL Renewable Energy credit grew 34.10% YoY in the current period, measured in ₹L Cr.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.direction: Growth rate is unchanged at 34.10% from the prior period (delta +0.00pp), holding at the maximum level observed across the 3-period historical window, with 'active' status indicating stable momentum.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.inference: Renewable Energy PSL is growing at 34% YoY, driven by India's energy transition agenda and favourable PSL classification for solar/wind projects. The 'active' rather than 'strengthening' status suggests growth is strong but not accelerating, likely constrained by project pipeline maturity and long gestation periods in infrastructure lending.

sibc-psl-yoy-scan.observation: Across 10 PSL sub-categories, 4 are strengthening, 4 active, and 2 declining; the spread between top (Housing 39.8%) and bottom (Others -9.6%) performers is 49.4 percentage points.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.direction: Housing (39.8%, strengthening), Renewable Energy (34.1%, active), and Micro & Small Enterprises (29.5%, strengthening) lead growth, while Others (-9.6%, declining) and Export Credit (-8.4%, declining) are contracting; Weaker Sections trails at 11.1% (active).
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.inference: PSL portfolio growth is highly concentrated in Housing, Renewable Energy, and MSME, which together drive the aggregate expansion. The sharp 49.4pp spread reveals structural reallocation: banks are prioritising secured retail (Housing) and policy-backed segments (MSME, Renewables) while retreating from Export Credit (trade slowdown) and miscellaneous 'Others'. Weaker Sections growth at 11.1% lags the PSL average, signalling potential regulatory compliance risk if banks undershoot sub-targets. Export Credit and Others categories are standout laggards, with Export Credit's -8.4% decline reflecting subdued global trade and reduced working capital demand from exporters.
